The PKGBUILD's global scope only contains variable assignments (pkgname, pkgver, source, etc.) with no command substitutions, backticks, or function calls that would execute during sourcing. No code outside the `package()` function exists, and that function is not executed under `--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`. Therefore, running the requested commands is safe.
</details>
<summary>No malicious top-level code; safe to source and fetch.</summary>

audits.body:
Cloning https://aur.archlinux.org/packettracer.git to /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-46236i6s...

Analyzing packettracer AUR package (working in /home/mgalgs/aur-sleuth-data/bulk-reports/qwen-qwen3-235b-a22b-2507/aur-sleuth-46236i6s) with qwen/qwen3-235b-a22b-2507 from https://openrouter.ai/api/v1
Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD defines a single source entry pointing to a local file: `local://CiscoPacketTracer_900_Ubuntu_64bit.deb`. This means `makepkg` will not attempt to download any remote content during the source fetch phase—it will only look for the specified `.deb` file in the local directory. No remote fetch commands (like `git clone`, `curl`, or `wget`) are triggered by sourcing this PKGBUILD.

The only executable code executed during `makepkg --nobuild --nodeps --noprepare` and `makepkg --printsrcinfo` is in the global scope and any `pkgver()` function. In this file, there is no `pkgver()` function, and the global scope contains only safe, declarative variable assignments (e.g., `pkgname`, `pkgver`, `source`). There is no command substitution, no network access, and no data exfiltration during sourcing.

The `package()` function is not executed due to `--nobuild --noprepare`, so operations like `tar xf` and `mv` are not run at this stage. Therefore, sourcing and parsing this PKGBUILD poses no risk of executing malicious code or leaking system information.
</details>
<summary>
No remote sources or malicious code in parse phase
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- No remote sources or malicious code in parse phase[/]

Performing initial audit of PKGBUILD to ensure that we can safely run `makepkg`
LLM auditresponse for PKGBUILD:
<security_assessment>
<decision>SAFE</decision>
<details>
The PKGBUILD is straightforward and minimal. The `source` array uses a `local://` URI, meaning it expects the user to have manually placed the Cisco Packet Tracer `.deb` file in the build directory — no network download occurs at all during sourcing. There is no top-level command substitution, no `pkgver()` function, and no code that executes during the parsing/sourcing phase. The sha512sum is provided for integrity verification of the local file.

All substantive operations (`tar`, `chown`, `mv`) are inside `package()`, which is not executed by the commands under review (`--nobuild --nodeps --noprepare`).
</details>
<summary>
Local-only source, no code runs during sourcing phase.
</summary>
</security_assessment>

+ Initial PKGBUILD audit complete [grey50]-- Local-only source, no code runs during sourcing phase.[/]

data.reasoning: Both audits failed at source download due to missing local .deb file, preventing review of critical installation scripts and binaries. No actual package contents were examined.
coverage_issues: Entire source package (CiscoPacketTracer_900_Ubuntu_64bit.deb) skipped due to makepkg failure
No review of .deb contents: installation scripts, binaries, or runtime behavior
false_negatives: Potential risks in unexamined .deb: post-install scripts, SUID binaries, or network services
PKGBUILD package() function (file operations) not executed during audit
learnings: Improve local source handling: Pre-validate existence of local:// files before makepkg
Add fallback workflow: Alert user to manually provide proprietary binaries for audit
Prioritize review of PKGBUILD functions over source when extraction fails
